title: Airway Management & Waveform Capnography
learningObjectives: Compare bag-valve-mask (BVM) ventilation, supraglottic airway (SGA) insertion, and endotracheal (ET) intubation as advanced airway options during cardiac arrest.
Describe the indications, advantages, and limitations of supraglottic airways (i-gel, King LT) in resuscitation.
Explain the appropriate timing of endotracheal intubation during CPR and the measures needed to minimize compression interruptions.
Interpret waveform capnography to confirm ET tube placement, monitor CPR quality, and detect ROSC.
State the ETCO2 target during CPR and the threshold value that suggests return of spontaneous circulation.
Describe how waveform capnography serves as the gold standard for advanced airway confirmation.
Integrate advanced airway management with the concurrent goals of high-quality CPR and rhythm-specific therapy.

summary.title: Advanced Airway Options in Cardiac Arrest
summary.content: During cardiac arrest, ACLS providers must secure an airway that allows effective ventilation while minimizing interruptions to chest compressions. The three primary options are bag-valve-mask (BVM) ventilation, supraglottic airways (SGAs) such as the i-gel and King LT, and endotracheal (ET) intubation. BVM with an oropharyngeal adjunct is the fastest to initiate and serves as the bridge to a more secure airway. SGAs are quicker to insert than an ET tube and require less skill to place, making them reasonable alternatives when intubation is not immediately available or when the intubation attempt would cause prolonged compression pauses. ET intubation provides the most definitive airway protection and allows precise ventilation control, but it requires experienced providers, causes compression interruptions during placement, and carries the risk of unrecognized esophageal intubation.

summary.title: Supraglottic Airways: i-gel and King LT
summary.content: The i-gel is a single-use SGA with a non-inflatable, gel-filled cushion that conforms to the perilaryngeal anatomy, requiring no cuff inflation. The King LT (and King LTS-D) is a dual-lumen SGA with a proximal and distal cuff that seals the esophagus and pharynx, directing gas flow through side ports into the glottis. Both devices can be inserted rapidly (typically under 15 seconds), do not require laryngoscopy, and provide a reasonable seal for positive-pressure ventilation. SGAs are particularly valuable in cardiac arrest because they allow relatively rapid airway establishment with minimal interruption of compressions. However, they do not provide absolute aspiration protection, and gastric insufflation can still occur. Position should be confirmed with waveform capnography after placement.

summary.title: Endotracheal Intubation During CPR
summary.content: When ET intubation is performed during cardiac arrest, the 2025 AHA Guidelines emphasize that it must be integrated without causing prolonged pauses in chest compressions. A single operator experienced in laryngoscopy should perform the attempt while another provider delivers continuous compressions using a mechanical device or a modified technique (e.g., jaw thrust without interrupting compressions). Video laryngoscopy is preferred over direct laryngoscopy when available because it may reduce first-pass failure and compression pauses. Each intubation attempt should not exceed 10 seconds. After placement, tube position must be confirmed immediately with waveform capnography. If ETCO2 is absent after intubation, the tube should be assumed to be esophageally placed and repositioned or replaced before ventilation continues.

summary.title: Waveform Capnography: The Gold Standard for Airway Confirmation
summary.content: Waveform capnography measures the concentration of carbon dioxide in exhaled breath and is the gold standard for confirming and continuously monitoring ET tube placement. Proper ET tube placement is confirmed by the presence of a characteristic rectangular waveform with a consistent ETCO2 reading on every breath cycle. The absence of a waveform after intubation strongly suggests esophageal placement, and the tube must be repositioned or replaced. A sudden loss of waveform with persistent effort may indicate tube displacement, obstruction, or cardiac arrest. Continuous waveform capnography should be displayed on the monitor throughout resuscitation once an advanced airway is in place.

summary.title: ETCO2 During CPR: Monitoring Quality and Detecting ROSC
summary.content: During cardiac arrest, ETCO2 reflects cardiac output because CO2 delivery to the lungs depends on blood flow generated by chest compressions. The 2025 AHA Guidelines identify an ETCO2 target of at least 10 mmHg during CPR as a marker of adequate compression quality. An ETCO2 persistently below 10 mmHg despite correct compression technique suggests inadequate compression rate, depth, recoil, or fraction, and should prompt reassessment of CPR quality. A sudden, sustained rise in ETCO2 — typically to values at or above 40 mmHg — during CPR is a reliable indicator of return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) because the restoration of native cardiac output delivers substantially more CO2 to the lungs. This early ROSC detection allows the team to immediately transition to post-arrest care without waiting for a manual pulse check.

summary.title: Integrating Airway Management with High-Quality CPR
summary.content: Advanced airway management during cardiac arrest must never become the centerpiece of the resuscitation at the expense of compression quality. The correct sequence is: (1) begin and maintain high-quality BVM ventilation as part of BLS; (2) when an advanced airway provider is ready, insert the chosen device during compressions with minimal interruption; (3) confirm placement with waveform capnography; (4) continue CPR with continuous waveform capnography monitoring; (5) use ETCO2 values to guide compression quality and detect ROSC. At no point should the team pause compressions to troubleshoot ventilation for an extended period. If an SGA or ET tube is not successfully placed on the first attempt, immediately return to BVM ventilation and high-quality CPR before reattempting.

clinicalPearls: Waveform capnography is the gold standard for ET tube placement confirmation — auscultation alone is insufficient during cardiac arrest.
ETCO2 persistently below 10 mmHg during CPR indicates poor compression quality and should trigger immediate reassessment of rate, depth, recoil, and fraction.
A sudden, sustained ETCO2 rise to 40 mmHg or above during CPR is a reliable indicator of ROSC — anticipate the pulse check and transition to post-arrest care.
Supraglottic airways are faster to place than ET tubes and cause fewer compression interruptions — they are reasonable alternatives when intubation is not immediately feasible.
Video laryngoscopy is preferred over direct laryngoscopy during cardiac arrest because it may reduce first-pass failure and compression pause duration.
Each intubation attempt should not exceed 10 seconds to minimize compression interruption.
redFlags: Absence of a capnography waveform after intubation — this is esophageal placement until proven otherwise.
ETCO2 persistently below 10 mmHg without reassessment of compression quality.
Extended compression pauses (>10 seconds) during intubation attempts or SGA placement.
Loss of the capnography waveform without clinical explanation — suspect tube displacement or obstruction.
Relying on auscultation and chest rise alone to confirm tube position without capnography.
commonMistakes: Prolonging compression pauses beyond 10 seconds for intubation attempts without adequate justification.
Failing to confirm ET tube placement with waveform capnography after every insertion attempt.
Over-ventilating after advanced airway placement, which can cause gastric insufflation and reduce coronary perfusion pressure.
Not using capnography to monitor CPR quality throughout the resuscitation, missing an opportunity to optimize compressions.
Attempting repeated intubation without returning to BVM ventilation between attempts.
memoryAids: '10-40-10 rule': ETCO2 below 10 mmHg = poor compressions; sudden rise above 40 mmHg = probable ROSC; every attempt under 10 seconds.
Waveform = yes = tube is tracheal; no waveform = tube is esophageal.
SGA first, tube if ready — supraglottic airways are fast and effective; ET intubation when the experienced provider can do it without long pauses.
Capnography watches the compressions for you — it tells you how hard you're pushing, not just where the tube is.

decisionTrees.title: Advanced Airway Decision During CPR
decisionTrees.content:
Cardiac arrest in progress, BVM ventilation underway
  → Is an experienced intubation provider available without delaying compressions?
     → YES → Attempt ET intubation during continuous compressions
        → Attempt <10 seconds, confirm with waveform capnography
        → Waveform present → Continue CPR with continuous capnography
        → No waveform → Assume esophageal placement, reposition, return to BVM between attempts
     → NO → Insert supraglottic airway (i-gel or King LT) during compressions
        → Confirm with waveform capnography
        → Waveform present → Continue CPR with continuous capnography
        → No waveform → Reposition or replace; return to BVM between attempts
  → Once advanced airway secured:
     → Confirm with continuous waveform capnography
     → Target ETCO2 ≥10 mmHg (compression quality goal)
     → Sudden ETCO2 rise ≥40 mmHg → suspect ROSC → pulse check

decisionTrees.title: ETCO2-Guided CPR Quality Feedback Loop
decisionTrees.content:
Waveform capnography active during CPR
  → ETCO2 ≥10 mmHg → Compressions are likely adequate; continue current technique
  → ETCO2 persistently <10 mmHg → Reassess CPR quality:
     → Rate 100–120/min?
     → Depth ≥2 inches (5 cm) in adults?
     → Full chest recoil between compressions?
     → Compression fraction ≥80%?
     → Correct any deficiency and reassess ETCO2
  → Sudden sustained ETCO2 ≥40 mmHg → Possible ROSC → Manual pulse check → If pulse present → Transition to post-arrest care

references: AHA 2025 Guidelines for CPR and ECC — Part 9: Adult Advanced Life Support
AHA 2025 Guidelines for CPR and ECC — Part 5: Adult BLS and CPR Quality
AHA 2025 Guidelines for CPR and ECC — Executive Summary: Airway Management and Ventilation

graphics.caption: Schematic diagram of five capnography waveforms: Normal, Hyperventilation, Hypoventilation, Esophageal Intubation (flat line), and ROSC (sudden spike).
